lessons_accepted.lesson: Prefer a concrete, visually obvious physical property over translated wordplay or an added social metaphor.
lessons_accepted.evidence: Loop 01’s echo was judged portable but mild; loop 02’s self-critique says a physical object failing at an ordinary task is more portable. Panel judges also described shadow and echo as gentle observations.
lessons_accepted.how_applied: Selected adjacent walls physically meeting at a corner, so the comic reveal is visible geometry rather than grammar.

lessons_accepted.lesson: Use everyday native vocabulary instead of technically correct but register-marked terms.
lessons_accepted.evidence: Loop 01 flagged Japanese こだま as less everyday; loop 02 improved to common 影 and 隠れる.
lessons_accepted.how_applied: Used ordinary wall/corner phrases and separately optimized Spanish rincón and colloquial Japanese 壁が隣の壁に何て言う.

lessons_rejected.lesson: Carry the shadow/follows-someone candidate into synthesis.
lessons_rejected.evidence: The three blind rankings placed A third, fourth, and third; panel feedback repeatedly called its payoff mild or more riddle-like than groan-inducing.
lessons_rejected.why: It is exceptionally portable, but the majority-preferred wall mechanism has a sharper Q&A reveal and stronger dad-joke groan.

lessons_accepted.lesson: Combine a classic Q&A rhythm with personification grounded in universal geometry.
lessons_accepted.evidence: Loop 01 identified the eyebrow narrative’s weaker interactive rhythm; loop 02 moved to walls/corner. Claude and Terra both ranked C first for its compact classic structure.
lessons_accepted.how_applied: Retained the walls/corner engine but rewrote every language instead of copying the parent text.

lessons_accepted.lesson: Visual mechanisms deserve serious weight even when they are not Q&A jokes.
lessons_accepted.evidence: Gemini’s blind judge ranked F first and called it a perfect visual dad joke; the other judges also treated F as a strong contender.
lessons_accepted.how_applied: Required the revised wall punchline to remain immediately visual, not merely idiomatic.

lessons_rejected.lesson: Treat the loop-02 translations as already perfect.
lessons_rejected.evidence: The generator self-scored translation naturalness 5/5, but Claude flagged Spanish esquina versus inner rincón, and Terra flagged stilted Japanese wall counting plus unnecessary quotation marks.
lessons_rejected.why: Independent panel evidence identified concrete seams; synthesis changes Spanish to rincón and rewrites Japanese without numeric counting or quotation marks.

lessons_rejected.lesson: Replace the wall mechanism with the eyebrow wording unchanged.
lessons_rejected.evidence: Gemini preferred F, but Claude and Terra preferred C; both noted F’s longer narrative, and Terra noted looser pun-like wording in French and Spanish.
lessons_rejected.why: The dissent is preserved, but C offers the better majority-supported balance of brevity and classic groan once its translation seams are repaired.

lessons_accepted.lesson: Audit the exact idiomatic and grammatical trigger in every target language rather than trusting semantic translation.
lessons_accepted.evidence: Loop 01 exposed avoir faim/tener hambre and the full Japanese hunger clause; loop 02’s durable lesson explicitly requires per-language grammatical auditing.
lessons_accepted.how_applied: Audited corner vocabulary by function: Spanish uses inner-corner rincón, while Japanese uses a colloquial neighbor-wall setup and no copied quote punctuation.

lessons_rejected.lesson: A shared copular adjective makes the tired/name joke naturally equivalent.
lessons_rejected.evidence: All three panel judges rejected E: Claude called the adjective-as-name forms odd and Japanese a failure; Terra called the replies literal calques; Gemini said the mechanism does not work naturally in Spanish or Japanese.
lessons_rejected.why: Matching surface grammar does not create a native comic license to treat an adjective or predicate as a name.

lessons_rejected.lesson: Accept the parent’s loop-02 provisional 5/5 naturalness claim.
lessons_rejected.evidence: The blind panel specifically identified “Salut Fatigué,” “Hola Cansado,” and “はじめまして疲れた” as unnatural or calque-like.
lessons_rejected.why: Independent evaluation outweighs generator self-scoring; only the audit discipline, not the candidate, transfers to synthesis.
